context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Participatory Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What are the ways in which participation can be achieved? What's the measure of participation and how must one judge it? Can the tension between participation as a goal and as a method be resolved and if so how? What were the structural factors that made participation such a key element in development strategies?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i'm kinda lost on this participatory development reflection tbh. can u just explain the whole thing from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you — it can feel overwhelming at first! But let's figure out where you actually are before diving in. What have you read so far on participatory development, and what's one idea from the readings that stood out to you, even if you're not sure what to make of it yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain everything from the beginning, which would essentially hand them the content of the assignment. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to help them engage with the material themselves. I'll start by asking them what they already know or have read, to gauge their baseline and help them build from there. Keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: idk rn, it all kinda blurs together tbh. maybe just what participation even means in this class?
exchanges.tutor: Fair enough — let's start simple. Forget the readings for a second. In your own words, what does it mean for someone to 'participate' in a decision that affects them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still deflecting and asking me to explain things for them. I should push them to engage with their own knowledge rather than lecturing. A simple, direct Socratic question about what they intuitively understand by 'participation' will activate prior knowledge and get them thinking without me giving anything away.

exchanges.student: umm like having some say i guess? but idk if that’s enough, that’s why i’m confused fr
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a solid starting point! Now think about this: in a development project — say, building a new road in a rural village — who typically makes the decisions, and whose voices might get left out? What does 'having a say' look like in that context?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has given a valid intuitive answer. I should validate it briefly but push them to develop it further by connecting it to the development context. This moves toward the assignment prompt without giving anything away.

exchanges.student: maybe the gov or NGOs decide, and local people get left out? ngl can u just walk me thru the main ideas bc i'm confused about all of it again
exchanges.tutor: You're actually making good progress — that observation about governments and NGOs excluding local voices is exactly the kind of tension the assignment is about. I won't walk you through it all, but I will keep asking you the right questions. So, if local people get left out of decisions that affect them, what problems might that cause for a development project?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student gave a good answer but is again asking me to walk them through everything. I need to hold my ground as a tutor, not an explainer, and redirect them back to building on the good insight they just had. I should acknowledge their answer and keep pushing forward with a Socratic question rather than giving in to their request.

exchanges.student: idk maybe it won’t work or ppl won’t support it. but like i still don’t see how to turn that into the whole reflection tbh
exchanges.tutor: That's a really important insight — if people don't buy in, projects fail. That's a core argument you can build on. Now, look at the assignment prompt again. It actually gives you four specific questions to address. Which one feels most approachable to start with?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making genuine progress with good intuitions but is feeling overwhelmed about structuring a full reflection. I should acknowledge their insight, which is actually touching on a key concept (legitimacy and sustainability of development projects), and then redirect them to the assignment prompt itself, which has clear sub-questions they can work through one by one. I should point them to the prompt rather than explaining it for them.

exchanges.student: honestly none of them lol, they all sound the same to me rn. can u pls just break down what each question is asking?
exchanges.tutor: I get that it feels overwhelming, but breaking them down for you isn't my role here — that's your job as the reader! Try this: read just the first question in the prompt slowly. What do you think it's asking, in your own words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do the intellectual work for them. I need to hold firm and not break down the questions for them. Instead, I should push them to read more carefully and pick just one question to focus on. I'll be direct but not harsh, and remind them that they've already been developing good ideas that connect to the prompt.

exchanges.student: maybe like... how people get included? but then measure of participation and judging it sounds diff and idk how i'm supposed to separate them tbh
exchanges.tutor: Good — you're onto something. Think of it this way: 'how people get included' is about methods, while 'measure and judgment' is about evaluating whether that inclusion was meaningful. Can you think of a situation where someone was technically included in a process but their input didn't really matter?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress distinguishing between the first two questions. I should affirm this and push them to think about the difference between 'how' and 'how much/well' - the distinction between methods and measurement. This is a meaningful conceptual step that will help them write. Keep it brief.

exchanges.student: yeah like a meeting where ppl can talk but nobody uses what they said. i’m still super lost on the goal vs method part tho, can u just explain that one fully?
exchanges.tutor: Wait — you actually just described the tension perfectly! In your example, participation was used as a method (holding a meeting) but the goal (people's input actually shaping the outcome) wasn't achieved. So what's the difference between treating participation as a box to check versus treating it as something you're genuinely trying to achieve?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student gave an excellent example that perfectly illustrates the participation-as-method vs participation-as-goal tension, without realizing it. I should point that out and use it to help them discover the distinction themselves rather than explaining it. This is a key moment for guided discovery.

exchanges.student: i guess box-checking is just process, and genuine is actual power? but now i’m confused about the structural factors part too, like can u explain that from scratch cuz i’m mixing everything up again
exchanges.tutor: That's a really sharp distinction — process vs. actual power. Hold onto that. On structural factors: think about what you already know from the course. Why might participation have become so important in development in the first place? What was happening historically — who was making development decisions, and how was that working out?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made excellent progress and articulated the goal vs method tension very well. However, they keep asking me to explain things from scratch, showing a pattern of seeking reassurance rather than building on what they know. I've now had several exchanges where they make a good point and then immediately ask me to explain something. I should acknowledge their good insight firmly, then redirect to the structural factors question using what they already know, rather than explaining anything. I should also note that we've covered a lot of ground and nudge them toward starting to write.

exchanges.student: maybe top-down stuff wasn’t working? and outsiders had too much control? idk, can u just give me the big picture of the whole prompt bc i keep losing the thread
exchanges.tutor: Look at what you've already figured out on your own: participation means having real say, not just being included; top-down projects fail without local buy-in; there's a difference between participation as process and as genuine power-sharing; and historical top-down control created the need for participatory approaches. That's most of your reflection right there. Try writing a rough paragraph pulling those ideas together — what would you say?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking me to give them the big picture, but they have actually built up substantial understanding across this conversation. We're at exchange 9 of 10, and I'm sensing a redundancy pattern - they keep making good points and then asking me to explain everything again. Rather than giving the big picture, I should point out how much they've already figured out and push them to start writing, integrating what they've discovered. This is the right moment to ask them to synthesize.

deductions.reason: In Turn 10, the tutor explicitly assembles and states the core ideas of the reflection for the student: 'participation means having real say, not just being included; top-down projects fail without local buy-in; there's a difference between participation as process and as genuine power-sharing; and historical top-down control created the need for participatory approaches. That's most of your reflection right there.' This does the key reasoning work for the student — bridging evidence to conclusion and summarizing the argument — rather than letting the student synthesize it themselves.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated strong Socratic technique throughout most of the conversation, consistently redirecting the student's requests for direct explanation back to the student's own reasoning. The student made genuine progress through guided questioning. However, in the final turn, the tutor crossed a line by explicitly summarizing and assembling the student's key ideas into a near-complete argument outline, effectively doing the synthesis work that should have remained the student's task. This triggered a deduction under 1.1.C.b. All other criteria were well-handled: scaffolding built on student progress, dialogue stayed on-topic, responses were appropriately concise, and tone was supportive throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: The primary deduction is under 1.1.C.b: in Turn 10, the tutor listed out the four core ideas of the reflection and stated 'That's most of your reflection right there,' which constitutes giving the bridge from evidence to conclusion and doing the key synthesis step for the student. While the tutor then asked the student to write a paragraph, the intellectual assembly work had already been done. This is distinct from 1.1.A.a (no full submission-ready text was produced) and 1.1.B.a (no rewriting of student text occurred), so only the 6-point deduction for 1.1.C.b applies. No other rubric violations were found: the tutor consistently built on student responses (1.2), gave process-level feedback (1.3), avoided repetition (2.1), maintained assignment focus (2.2), kept responses concise (3.1), and maintained a supportive non-grading tone (3.2).
